title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an etnobotanist called Dr. Amara Smith talking about her research into traditional plant-based medicines. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an etnobotanist called Dr. Amara Smith talking about her research into traditional plant-based medicines. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Dr. Amara Smith discusses her work with indigenous communities to document the uses of local flora.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. My name is Dr. Amara Smith and I’m an ethnobotanist, which means I study the relationship between people and plants. My work often takes me to the most remote corners of the world. [7]I first became fascinated by this field after watching a documentary about the healing properties of rainforest plants[/7]. It opened my eyes to the vast library of knowledge held by indigenous cultures. My recent project involved living with a community in the Andes. [8]I found that the process of building trust with the local elders was the most rewarding part of the experience[/8]. They are the keepers of secrets that have been passed down for generations. One of the plants we studied is used to treat skin infections. [9]The extraction of the active ingredient from the purple bark was a very delicate procedure[/9], requiring specific temperatures. It’s not just about the plants, but how they are prepared. While in the field, I had to adapt to a very different lifestyle. [10]The lack of reliable electricity meant that I had to do all my note-taking by candlelight[/10]. It was quite atmospheric, if a bit hard on the eyes. The community was incredibly generous, and [11]I was surprised to find that their sense of humor was a great way to bridge the cultural gap[/11]. We laughed a lot, even when we didn't speak the same language. One major challenge we face is environmental. [12]The rapid rate of deforestation is the biggest threat to our research[/12], as many plants are disappearing before we can even name them. It’s a race against time. Looking forward, [13]I believe that benefit-sharing agreements are essential for the future of etnobotany[/13]. We must ensure that the communities own the rights to their knowledge. As I often tell my students, [14]we are not just collectors, we are 'guardians of wisdom' for the natural world[/14]. It’s a responsibility I take very seriously.

dialogue.speaker: amara
dialogue.text: Hello. My name is Dr. Amara Smith and I’m an ethnobotanist, which means I study the relationship between people and plants. My work often takes me to the most remote corners of the world. I first became fascinated by this field after watching a documentary about the healing properties of rainforest plants. It opened my eyes to the vast library of knowledge held by indigenous cultures. My recent project involved living with a community in the Andes. I found that the process of building trust with the local elders was the most rewarding part of the experience. They are the keepers of secrets that have been passed down for generations. One of the plants we studied is used to treat skin infections. The extraction of the active ingredient from the purple bark was a very delicate procedure, requiring specific temperatures. It’s not just about the plants, but how they are prepared. While in the field, I had to adapt to a very different lifestyle. The lack of reliable electricity meant that I had to do all my note-taking by candlelight. It was quite atmospheric, if a bit hard on the eyes. The community was incredibly generous, and I was surprised to find that their sense of humor was a great way to bridge the cultural gap. We laughed a lot, even when we didn't speak the same language. One major challenge we face is environmental. The rapid rate of deforestation is the biggest threat to our research, as many plants are disappearing before we can even name them. It’s a race against time. Looking forward, I believe that benefit-sharing agreements are essential for the future of etnobotany. We must ensure that the communities own the rights to their knowledge. As I often tell my students, we are not just collectors, we are 'guardians of wisdom' for the natural world. It’s a responsibility I take very seriously.

questions.question: Amara’s interest in ethnobotany was first sparked by a (7) she saw.
questions.explanation: She mentions watching a documentary about rainforest plants.

questions.question: Amara considers the time spent (8) as the highlight of her time in the Andes.
questions.explanation: She says building trust with local elders was the most rewarding part.

questions.question: Amara describes the task of obtaining the chemical from the (9) as a difficult process.
questions.explanation: The extraction from the purple bark was a delicate procedure.

questions.question: Amara notes that she had to rely on (10) in order to write down her observations.
questions.explanation: Due to the lack of electricity, she did her note-taking by candlelight.

questions.question: According to Amara, the locals' (11) helped her connect with them more easily.
questions.explanation: She found their sense of humor was a great way to bridge the cultural gap.

questions.question: Amara identifies (12) as the most significant problem facing her field of study.
questions.explanation: She explicitly states that the rapid rate of deforestation is the biggest threat.

questions.question: Amara argues that (13) must be established to protect indigenous intellectual property.
questions.explanation: She believes benefit-sharing agreements are essential for the future.

questions.question: Amara uses the phrase (14) to explain the deeper meaning of her profession.
questions.explanation: She says ethnobotanists are 'guardians of wisdom' for the natural world.
